Patch Set 1:

doesn't this duplicate slab allocator?

Patch Set 1:

> doesn't this duplicate slab allocator?

Hi, Alex, Could you please point out the detail location?

Patch Set 1:

> Hi, Alex, Could you please point out the detail location?

slab saves just freed chunks in per-cpu cache, so reallocation is very fast and cheap if you create your own slab with kmem_cache_create()

Patch Set 1:

> > Hi, Alex, Could you please point out the detail location?
 > 
 > slab saves just freed chunks in per-cpu cache, so reallocation is
 > very fast and cheap if you create your own slab with
 > kmem_cache_create()

But it may case sleep while allocation. So we have to release the res lock when we do so.

Patch Set 1:

> But it may case sleep while allocation. So we have to release the res lock when we do so.

GFP_ATOMIC

I'm not a big fan of own "free" lists as the regular VM can't reuse them.

Patch Set 1:

> But it may case sleep while allocation. So we have to release the res lock when we do so.

are you saying your "free list" is always populated?

Patch Set 1:

> > But it may case sleep while allocation. So we have to release the
 > res lock when we do so.
 > 
 > are you saying your "free list" is always populated?

No, It will be released when ldlm_lock destroy.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

This patch is hard to understand.  As well as doing the thing that is described in the commit message (which may well be a sensible thing to do), it refactors a lot of code.  That makes it hard to follow.  Refactoring the code is probably a good idea.  Avoiding the allocation is probably a good idea.  Doing them both in the same patch is a bad idea.
Storing the spare node in struct ldlm_interval is also a bad idea.  It wastes 2 pointer which are hardly every used.  It could be easily changed to only waste one pointer - a single pointer to the allocated node - but even that isn't necessary.

If we ignore all the refactoring, I would suggest changing ldlm_resource_unlink_lock() to take a second argument - a 'struct ldlm_interval **' (or maybe a void** if that avoid extra #includes).  If this is NULL, it does exactly what it does now.  This pointer is passed to ldlm_extent_unlink_lock().  If it is not NULL, then ldlm_extent_unlink_lock doesn't call ldlm_interval_free() but instead stores the node in the location pointed to.

ldlm_lock_enqueue never tries to allocate 'node' but instead passes &node as the new arg to ldlm_resource_unlink_lock() (All other callers leave it as NULL).  This way it will have a useful value when ldlm_interval_attach() is called.

That should be all that is needed.

If you want to refactor the code in a separate patch, please do.  But keep that separate from the change in behaviour.

Patch Set 1:

> This patch is hard to understand.  As well as doing the thing that
 > is described in the commit message (which may well be a sensible
 > thing to do), it refactors a lot of code.  That makes it hard to
 > follow.  Refactoring the code is probably a good idea.  Avoiding
 > the allocation is probably a good idea.  Doing them both in the
 > same patch is a bad idea.
It is not a patch for refactoring & allocation. The all of part is related. So we have to change it in one patch.


 > Storing the spare node in struct ldlm_interval is also a bad idea. 
 > It wastes 2 pointer which are hardly every used.  It could be
 > easily changed to only waste one pointer - a single pointer to the
 > allocated node - but even that isn't necessary.
It is also not one pointer or two pointer things. The ldlm_interval buffer could be more since we could enqueue many locks with same range for extent lock and flock. So we have to use a list to store them.

 > 
 > If we ignore all the refactoring, I would suggest changing
 > ldlm_resource_unlink_lock() to take a second argument - a 'struct
 > ldlm_interval **' (or maybe a void** if that avoid extra
 > #includes).  If this is NULL, it does exactly what it does now. 
 > This pointer is passed to ldlm_extent_unlink_lock().  If it is not
 > NULL, then ldlm_extent_unlink_lock doesn't call ldlm_interval_free()
 > but instead stores the node in the location pointed to.
 > 
No, The problem is not in ldlm_extent_unlink_lock, but in ldlm_extent_add_lock. The ldlm_interval buffer will be freed in latter. 


 > ldlm_lock_enqueue never tries to allocate 'node' but instead passes
 > &node as the new arg to ldlm_resource_unlink_lock() (All other
 > callers leave it as NULL).  This way it will have a useful value
 > when ldlm_interval_attach() is called.
 > 
If we can guarantee all of lock always has a ldlm_interval buffer. Then this part should not be necessary.

 > That should be all that is needed.
 > 
 > If you want to refactor the code in a separate patch, please do. 
 > But keep that separate from the change in behaviour.
I have to repeat it is not a separate things.

Patch Set 1:

> It is also not one pointer or two pointer things. The ldlm_interval buffer could be more since we could enqueue many locks with same range for extent lock and flock. So we have to use a list to store them.

In that case the comment for li_free is wrong.  It should be "Free buffers" - plural.

There are places where you take an element off the list without testing if the list is empty.
That means you "know" it cannot be empty.  You need to document the rules for how li_free is used.  When can it be empty?  How many entries can it have.  It seems to be related to li_group, but it isn't clear how.

You've changed ldlm_extent_alloc_lock (by changing ldlm_interval_alloc) so that the interval is not on l_sl_policy any more - but you haven't explained why.

For me, this patch is too hard to review.  It needs to either be a lot simpler, to have much better description of what it is changing, and why.

Patch Set 1:

> > It is also not one pointer or two pointer things. The
 > ldlm_interval buffer could be more since we could enqueue many
 > locks with same range for extent lock and flock. So we have to use
 > a list to store them.
 > 
 > In that case the comment for li_free is wrong.  It should be "Free
 > buffers" - plural.
 > 
Ok, My english is really not so good. 

 > There are places where you take an element off the list without
 > testing if the list is empty.
 > That means you "know" it cannot be empty.  You need to document the
 > rules for how li_free is used.  When can it be empty?  How many
 > entries can it have.  It seems to be related to li_group, but it
 > isn't clear how.
 > 
We needn't test it every time. For correctness i assert it while it should be empty.

 > You've changed ldlm_extent_alloc_lock (by changing
 > ldlm_interval_alloc) so that the interval is not on l_sl_policy any
 > more - but you haven't explained why.
 > 
Could you please point out which line make you think that interval is not on l_sl_policy? In fact, It should be described l_sl_policy on interval_node. 


 > For me, this patch is too hard to review.  It needs to either be a
 > lot simpler, to have much better description of what it is
 > changing, and why.

Patch Set 1:

I think I've work out what this patch is doing.

The current code allocates ->l_tree_node for any extent lock when the lock is created.  This is used to link the lock into the extent tree.  If two locks with identical extents are in the tree then one of the ->l_tree_nodes is discarded and the remaining one is shared between the locks.
However is a lock is removed from the extent tree but not immediately destroyed, then it won't have a ->l_tree_node which is bad, so one has to be allocated.  This is clumsy.

With the new code we never free the ->l_tree_node until the lock itself is destroyed.  Instead when two locks in the extent tree have the same extent there is a "main" ldlm_interval which all the locks refer to with their ->l_tree_node and all the other ldlm_intervals are linked together with the ->l_free list.  Whenever a lock that shares an ldlm_interval is detached from the interval tree, the first l_free ldlm_interval is removed from the free list. and attach to the lock.

So it makes sense and is probably correct.  It would a lot to have the above description in the commit message.

However it still wastes space.  You don't need a list_head in each ldlm_interval - only a pointer to an ldlm_interval.  Maybe call it "struct ldlm_interval *l_next_free;".
The ldlm_interval that is in the interval tree points to a free ldlm_interval, and that one points to another free interval and so on.  We always insert at the top of list, and remove from the top of the list.

But it would be much much better to discard struct ldlm_interval completely and just allow locks with the same extent to each be independently in the interval tree.
https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/33221 does this. Maybe it is time to try to get that patch accepted again.

Patch Set 1:

> I think I've work out what this patch is doing.
 > 
 > The current code allocates ->l_tree_node for any extent lock when
 > the lock is created.  This is used to link the lock into the extent
 > tree.  If two locks with identical extents are in the tree then one
 > of the ->l_tree_nodes is discarded and the remaining one is shared
 > between the locks.
 > However is a lock is removed from the extent tree but not
 > immediately destroyed, then it won't have a ->l_tree_node which is
 > bad, so one has to be allocated.  This is clumsy.
 > 
 > With the new code we never free the ->l_tree_node until the lock
 > itself is destroyed.  Instead when two locks in the extent tree
 > have the same extent there is a "main" ldlm_interval which all the
 > locks refer to with their ->l_tree_node and all the other
 > ldlm_intervals are linked together with the ->l_free list. 
 > Whenever a lock that shares an ldlm_interval is detached from the
 > interval tree, the first l_free ldlm_interval is removed from the
 > free list. and attach to the lock.
 > 
 > So it makes sense and is probably correct.  It would a lot to have
 > the above description in the commit message.
 > 
 > However it still wastes space.  You don't need a list_head in each
 > ldlm_interval - only a pointer to an ldlm_interval.  Maybe call it
 > "struct ldlm_interval *l_next_free;".
 > The ldlm_interval that is in the interval tree points to a free
 > ldlm_interval, and that one points to another free interval and so
 > on.  We always insert at the top of list, and remove from the top
 > of the list.
 > 
 > But it would be much much better to discard struct ldlm_interval
 > completely and just allow locks with the same extent to each be
 > independently in the interval tree.
 > https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/33221 does this.
 > Maybe it is time to try to get that patch accepted again.

Well, You got the patch doing. You said may patch waste space. But your patch more worse since it makes ldlm_lock larger, So it means alloc interval_node even for ibits & plain lock. It is unacceptable. Also do you have test results of performance for your patch. I suspect it cause bad performance when many of same extent locks exist in tree.

Patch Set 1:

> But your patch more worse since it makes ldlm_lock larger, So it means alloc interval_node even for ibits & plain lock. 

True.  My memory was that later patches removed the wastage though I cannot find that it removed all of it.  ldlm_lock is so large that only 7 fit in a page, though 15 would fit in a pair of pages.  It it grows at all, only 14 would git in a pair of pages.  I'll have a look at hat...

> Also do you have test results of performance for your patch. 

No - I don't really know how I would test performance.

> I suspect it cause bad performance when many of same extent locks exist in tree.

It shouldn't.  Extends are stored in a balanced tree so search length in only logarithmic in the number of elements.  And the simpler code from would likely give back some of the performance lost.
But I would be interested in seeing performance results...

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

This change cannot be cherry-picked to master.
Please rebase the change locally and upload the rebased commit for review.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

Can be abandon.
Abandoned

parents.subject: LU-17276 ldlm: add interval in flock

commit.subject: LU-17276 ldlm: don't release ldlm_interval buffer
commit.message:
LU-17276 ldlm: don't release ldlm_interval buffer

We will release the ldlm_interval buffer while insert
same extent in tree. But it may be reused late. We have
to alloc it again in such case. So just link it to interval
node and assign it back when the lock was remove from tree.

Signed-off-by: Yang Sheng <ys@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: I4e0277e0b869ed3b330f1402ae8d0601e72423f0
